prose: The evidence-ranked mechanistic hypothesis for acetaminophen is negative with a clean-negative tier, resting on the curated mechanistic record: there is no curated Parkinson's DirectEvidence linking this compound to disease-relevant pathways, and it is not a registered neuro-AOP stressor [E1]. That curated absence is the sole basis for the call. Corroborating, though never decisive, real-world pharmacovigilance shows zero parkinsonism signal across 668,109 FAERS reports, with a reporting odds ratio of 0.68 that is not elevated [E2]—an anti-diagnostic pattern consistent with, but not proof of, the negative mechanistic read. Brain-penetrance is plausible on structure-based prediction [E3], yet this is not a discriminating feature here, since penetration alone confers no mechanistic tie to neurodegeneration. Taken together, the curated mechanism drives a confident negative, with assay-adjacent and exposure strands offering only supportive context. This reflects hypothesis-ranking, not any claim of causation.

citations.detail: No curated Parkinson's DirectEvidence and not a registered neuro-AOP stressor.
citations.reference: Davis AP, Wiegers TC, Johnson RJ, et al. Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD): update 2023. Nucleic Acids Research. 2023;51(D1):D1257-D1262. Mechanistic scaffold: OECD Adverse Outcome Pathway Wiki. AOP 3: Inhibition of the mitochondrial complex I of nigro-striatal neurons leads to parkinsonian motor deficits (WPHA/WNT Endorsed).

citations.detail: Zero parkinsonism signal across 668109 FAERS reports (ROR 0.68, not elevated).
citations.reference: U.S. FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS), via the openFDA drug/event API. Disproportionality (ROR) for the parkinsonism MedDRA cluster.

citations.detail: Brain-penetrant, but penetrance is not a discriminator here.
citations.reference: Meng F, Xi Y, Huang J, Ayers PW. A curated diverse molecular database of blood-brain barrier permeability with chemical descriptors (B3DB). Scientific Data. 2021;8:289. Daina A, Zoete V. A BOILED-Egg to predict gastrointestinal absorption and brain access of small molecules. ChemMedChem. 2016;11(11):1117-1121.
